figures.gloss: The thirteenth-century Japanese prophet (1222-1282) whose Buddhism the Gakkai practices: convinced the Lotus Sutra alone held the truth for the degenerate age, he distilled its power into chanting its title, denounced every rival school, warned the nation in the Rissho ankoku ron (1260), survived a near-execution at Tatsunokuchi (1271) and two exiles, and identified himself with the sutra's Bodhisattva Superior Practices. The Nichiren Shoshu doctrine the Gakkai inherited venerates him as the True Buddha of the Latter Day.
figures.facet: The rare Buddhist founder whose signature mode is confrontation — remonstrating with governments, naming heresies, courting martyrdom — which is precisely the inheritance that made his twentieth-century lay revival both evangelically explosive and politically unafraid.
figures.provenance: Nichiren's own writings (Gosho): Rissho ankoku ron (1260), Kaimoku-sho (1272), Kanjin no honzon-sho (1273); standard Nichiren biography scholarship.

figures.gloss: The venerated founding lineage: Makiguchi Tsunesaburo (1871-1944), the educator whose 'value-creating' (soka) pedagogy named the movement and who died in prison after refusing the wartime state's Shinto talisman; Toda Josei (1900-1958), whose own prison awakening rebuilt the Gakkai into a mass movement and who declared against nuclear weapons in 1957; and Ikeda Daisaku (1928-2023), who globalized it as SGI (1975), founded universities, and launched the Komeito party (1964, formally separated from the Gakkai's religious organization in 1970).
figures.facet: Succession itself is the sacrament here: the 'oneness of mentor and disciple' between the three presidents functions as the movement's transmission lineage, doing the work a priestly ordination line does elsewhere — which is why the organization survived losing its priesthood entirely.
figures.provenance: Makiguchi, Soka kyoikugaku taikei (1930); Toda's postwar lectures and the 8 Sept 1957 anti-nuclear declaration; Ikeda, The Human Revolution; Levi McLaughlin, Soka Gakkai's Human Revolution (2019).

figures.gloss: The core practice: chanting 'Nam-myoho-renge-kyo' — devotion to the Mystic Law that is the Lotus Sutra's title — aloud, repeatedly, before the Gohonzon, morning and evening. In Nichiren's teaching the title contains the sutra's whole power, so the chant is held to draw out the chanter's innate Buddhahood and to change concrete circumstances: health, work, relationships are all legitimate objects of chanting.
figures.facet: Salvation compressed to a single repeatable utterance with no prerequisites — no ordination, no meditation training, no literacy in doctrine — which is the engine of the movement's mass scalability: the entire path fits in seven syllables anyone can say tonight.
figures.provenance: Nichiren's Gosho on the daimoku's supremacy; SGI liturgy (gongyo); McLaughlin (2019) on practice in members' lives.

figures.gloss: The object of devotion: a calligraphic mandala devised by Nichiren with 'Nam-myoho-renge-kyo' inscribed down the center and the beings of the ten worlds arrayed around it in written characters — the Lotus Sutra's Ceremony in the Air rendered entirely in script, with no image. Members enshrine a printed transcription at home and chant before it; since 1993, following the 1991 rupture with the priesthood, the Gakkai has conferred its own reproductions of a 1720 transcription by the reformer-priest Nichikan.
figures.facet: An aniconic altar made of language: where other Buddhisms center a statue, Nichiren centered a page — and SGI teaches it as a mirror that reflects the chanter's own Buddha-nature back, not a vessel containing power in itself. The 1993 self-issuance is the movement's declaration that no priesthood owns access to it.
figures.provenance: Nichiren's Kanjin no honzon-sho (1273) and surviving autograph mandalas; SGI's 1993 issuance of the Nichikan Gohonzon after Nichiren Shoshu's excommunication of the Gakkai (28 Nov 1991).

figures.gloss: The movement's summary doctrine, coined under Toda and canonized in Ikeda's novel-cycle of the same name: a profound inner transformation in a single ordinary person — won through practice — ripples outward to change a family, a society, and ultimately the destiny of humankind. It reframes Buddhist awakening as this-worldly self-transformation with public consequences, grounding the Gakkai's activism in education, culture, and peace work.
figures.facet: Enlightenment retooled as a theory of social change: the unit of world transformation is deliberately set at one unheroic individual, which dignifies every member's private struggle as cosmically consequential — the doctrinal core of the movement's morale.
figures.provenance: Toda's postwar usage; Ikeda Daisaku, The Human Revolution (serialized from 1965); McLaughlin (2019).

edges.note: The daimoku is Nichiren's signature act — he established chanting the Lotus Sutra's title as the whole practice for the Latter Day, and everything the Gakkai does descends from that compression.
The chant and its object are one apparatus: the Gohonzon is the daimoku written and enthroned, and the daimoku is the Gohonzon spoken — practice is performed aloud before the words it repeats.

The doctrine is told through the lineage: Ikeda's Human Revolution novels narrate Toda's (and then his own) transformation as the archetype every member reenacts — the presidents are the principle's worked examples.

BUDDHIST SEAM: Nichiren read himself into the Lotus Sutra as Bodhisattva Superior Practices, leader of the Bodhisattvas of the Earth, and SGI members take that role as their own collective identity — the Mahayana bodhisattva vow inherited as the movement's self-definition.

BUDDHIST SEAM: chanting works, in Nichiren doctrine, because Buddhahood is already innate and need only be called forth — the daimoku is the tathagatagarbha strand of Mahayana turned into a daily vocal technology.

A precise inversion of the root-teacher pattern: SGI abolished dependence on clergy altogether after the 1991 excommunication, yet re-centers devotion on the mentor-disciple bond with a lay president — the guru-function surviving, relocated outside priesthood entirely.

Two twentieth-century movements that carried a single sonic formula out of its monastic matrix and scaled it to a global lay membership — though the daimoku is chanted aloud as public devotion with doctrinal content, where the TM mantra is silent and deliberately semantically empty.

Two Japanese new religions relocating salvation into this world: Tenrikyo's Joyous Life and the Gakkai's human revolution both make the transformed ordinary life — not a hereafter — the religion's promised outcome; parallel Japanese NRM pattern, separate lineages.

dossier.essence: Japan's largest lay Buddhist movement and most prominent new religion: founded in 1930 by the educator Makiguchi Tsunesaburo as the Soka Kyoiku Gakkai ('Value-Creating Education Society'), rebuilt after the war by Toda Josei into a mass movement, and globalized by Ikeda Daisaku as Soka Gakkai International (1975). It practices the Buddhism of the thirteenth-century prophet Nichiren — chanting the Lotus Sutra's title as the complete path — and claims several million Japanese households plus members in 192 countries and territories.
dossier.cosmos: The worldview is Nichiren's reading of the Lotus Sutra: all ten realms of existence, Buddhahood included, are mutually present in every moment of life (the Tendai doctrine of three thousand realms in a single thought-moment), so enlightenment is innate and available now, in this body and this life, not after death or eons of practice. Chanting Nam-myoho-renge-kyo before the Gohonzon is held to draw out that innate Buddhahood and to transform karma and concrete circumstances alike. Death is framed through Toda's and Ikeda's 'eternity of life' teaching — life and death as alternating phases of one continuous life — rather than through a heaven or a final release.
dossier.practice: The daily discipline is gongyo: morning and evening recitation and sustained chanting of the daimoku before a home-enshrined Gohonzon, Nichiren's calligraphic mandala, with health, work, and relationships all legitimate objects of prayer. The social unit is the neighborhood discussion meeting (zadankai), and the ethic is 'human revolution' — inner transformation rippling outward — expressed through aggressive postwar proselytizing (shakubuku, since softened), the Komeito party Ikeda founded in 1964, and a dense apparatus of schools, universities, and peace and cultural institutions. Since Nichiren Shoshu excommunicated the organization in 1991 it has operated with no priesthood at all, conferring its own Gohonzon reproductions since 1993.
dossier.canon: Nichiren's collected writings (the Gosho) and the Lotus Sutra are the scriptural core; Ikeda's serial novel-cycle The Human Revolution functions as the movement's de facto supplementary canon of history and exemplars.
dossier.reflects: Salvation engineered for mass scalability — the whole path compressed into one repeatable utterance with no prerequisites, and this-worldly self-transformation organized into a modern, global lay institution.
sources: Levi McLaughlin, 'Soka Gakkai's Human Revolution: The Rise of a Mimetic Nation in Modern Japan' (2019)
Richard Hughes Seager, 'Encountering the Dharma: Daisaku Ikeda, Soka Gakkai, and the Globalization of Buddhist Humanism' (2006)

links.cite: Richard Hughes Seager, 'Encountering the Dharma: Daisaku Ikeda, Soka Gakkai, and the Globalization of Buddhist Humanism' (2006)
Levi McLaughlin, 'Soka Gakkai's Human Revolution: The Rise of a Mimetic Nation in Modern Japan' (2019)
